body:
## Current State

The `/api/user-photo` Azure Function is fully implemented and deployed. The frontend (`Header.tsx`) already calls it on load and displays the photo when available. The silent fallback to initials (e.g. "JC") works correctly.

## Why the photo doesn't load

The Graph API endpoint `GET /users/{email}/photo/$value` requires the user to exist in the **same Entra ID tenant** where the app registration lives. Currently:

- **Personal deployment** (`brave-wave`): The Entra ID tenant is backed by a free Outlook.com consumer account. Consumer Microsoft accounts don't have profile photos accessible via the Graph `/users/{email}/photo` endpoint — this endpoint only works for Microsoft 365 organizational accounts.
- **Employer deployment** (`mango-beach`): The user exists in the employer's M365 tenant, but the IT admin hasn't granted `User.Read.All` admin consent on the ProcureFlow app registration. Without admin consent, the client credentials token cannot read user photos.

This is a **platform limitation**, not a code bug.

## What's already done

- [x] `api/src/functions/user-photo.ts` — Client credentials flow, Graph token caching, photo proxy, silent 204 fallback
- [x] `Header.tsx` — `useEffect` calls `/api/user-photo`, displays blob URL if available, shows initials otherwise
- [x] YAML updated — `AZURE_TENANT_ID`, `AZURE_CLIENT_ID`, `AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET` provisioned as SWA app settings
- [x] `User.Read.All` application permission added to Entra ID app registration
- [x] Admin consent granted on personal tenant

## What's needed to activate

Deploy ProcureFlow to a tenant that has **Microsoft 365 Business** (or higher) licenses. Then:

1. Ensure the Entra ID app registration has `User.Read.All` **application** permission (already in YAML)
2. Grant admin consent: **Azure Portal → Entra ID → App registrations → ProcureFlow → API permissions → "Grant admin consent"**
3. Verify the three env vars are set on the SWA: `AZURE_TENANT_ID`, `AZURE_CLIENT_ID`, `AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET`
4. Users must have a profile photo set in their M365 account (https://myaccount.microsoft.com)

Once these conditions are met, photos will load automatically — no code changes required. The function caches the Graph token in memory and returns photos with a 1-hour browser cache header.

labels.name: Blocked
labels.description: Blocked by missing prerequisite (tenant, env, etc.). Do not work on until resolved.

title: Avatar: Enable Microsoft Graph profile photo (Phase 2 complete — awaiting M365 tenant)

body:
- Current logout clears local state but doesn't revoke Azure AD session  
- Result: 'Login with Microsoft' button auto-refreshes old token—no real sign-out  
- Repro: Log in → logout → press Microsoft button → still signed in as previous user  
- Fix: Call MSAL logoutRedirect() or logoutPopup() after clearing local tokens  
- Add loading spinner during redirect  
- Post-logout: force full auth prompt on next login  
- Test: Multi-user flow—Alice logs out, Bob signs in fresh
title: Bug: Logout doesn't revoke MS token—next user auto-logs in

body:
- Add delete option for POs (single or bulk select)  
- On delete: soft-delete (mark isDeleted: true + deletedAt timestamp), remove from main list, move to 'Trash' tab  
- Trash tab: read-only view—PO number, supplier, items, deleter, delete date; sortable/searchable  
- Auto-purge: daily job deletes >30 days old; log quietly  
- Restore: multi-select + 'Restore' button—revert isDeleted, clear deletedAt, restore linked receipts/statuses intact  
- Confirmation: 'Delete X POs? To trash (restorable 30 days)'—show count, no auto-delete force  
- Block delete if open/pending receipts: warn 'X receipts pending—delete anyway?'  
- Offline: queue actions, sync on reconnect  
- Audit log: track delete/restore with user + timestamp.



**Claude prompt:**  

Add soft-delete for purchase orders in Dost Lager, with a 30-day trash bin.  

Users select one or multiple POs in the list, tap 'Delete'—show a single confirmation: 'Delete X POs? They'll go to trash, restorable for 30 days. All linked receipts will be hidden too.' On confirm, mark as deleted (isDeleted: true, deletedAt: now), hide from active views.  

Create a 'Trash' tab/filter: read-only table showing deleted POs—number, supplier, item count, who deleted, when. No edits here.  

Add 'Restore' button for selected items: flip flags back, restore receipts and statuses exactly as before—no data loss. Show brief toast: 'Restored X POs' with 5-second undo link.  

Auto-delete anything older than 30 days via daily job; log it silently. Prevent delete on POs with pending receipts—gray out or warn. Queue offline deletes/restores, sync later.  

Test: delete 5 POs, check Trash tab, restore 3, verify receipts reappear unchanged, wait 30+ days for auto-purge. Leave other flows untouched.
title: Feature: Soft-delete POs with 30-day trash bin + bulk restore

body:
Error: 'supplier "attached" not in list' during bulk order import.
Repro: Upload txt with known-good suppliers (worked pre-redeploy) → fails post-test subscription deploy.
Check: validation logic, supplier DB sync, case-sensitivity, quotes/spaces.
Priority: High—blocks bulk testing.


Bulk import prompt, clean version:  

"During bulk order import, the system throws: 'supplier \"attached\" not in list'—even though the supplier exists in our list, the CSV is identical to one that succeeded pre-redeploy, and there are no typos, case mismatches, or extra spaces. Investigate:  
- Validation rule for supplier lookup (exact match? normalized?).  
- Database sync after subscription change—did the supplier table migrate correctly?  
- Quote handling in CSV parsing—does \"attached\" get stripped or escaped wrong?  
- Any new middleware or env var introduced in the test deployment that's altering input.  

Goal: import completes without error
title: Bug: Bulk import orders fails with 'supplier attached not in list' error despite valid suppliers

body:
- Panel shrinks unexpectedly when cursor hits left edge  
- Repro: Hover cursor fully left after test subscription redeploy  
- Priority: High  



For the Claude prompt:  

"Imagine opening Dost Lager on a test deployment: the side panel menu—your trusty navigation hub—should slide out like a calm drawer, steady and predictable. But now, it stutters, jumps, or shrinks when the cursor hits the left edge. Fix this glitch, ensuring smooth expansion without unexpected resizing.
title: Bug: Side panel shrinks on full left hover post-redeploy

body:
- In mobile Orders list, cards blend together—user loses track of where one order ends and next begins  
- No clear visual boundary: even in light/dark mode, hard to spot 'this is one complete order'  
- Feels like content continues underneath, or orders merge visually  
- Goal: make each card distinctly readable—stronger dividers, subtle shadows, better spacing, or accent colors so eye jumps naturally between them  
- Keep it subtle, no clutter—should 'click' instantly on first look  
- Test on real devices: scroll list fast, confirm no confusion about order separation.


**Claude prompt:**

Picture the mobile Orders screen: a vertical stack of cards, each one a purchase order. Right now, they feel like one long, blurry ribbon—your eye drifts down, but you can't quickly say, "Ah, this ends here, that one's next." The borders are too soft, spacing too tight, colors too similar. In light mode they wash out; in dark they merge into shadows. It doesn't matter how fast you scroll—you're guessing where one stops.

Make each card breathe like its own little island. Give it a gentle lift—maybe a hairline divider, a faint shadow that says "I'm separate," or a bolder header bar that snaps into focus. Keep the card compact, but let it own its space: top edge crisp, bottom edge clear, no bleed-over. The user should glance once and know: "Order 123—done. Order 456—next." No mental math.

Test it in both modes, on small phones—scroll, tap, flick through ten orders. Does your brain auto-separate them? If yes, perfect. If not, nudge the contrast or padding until it clicks. Nothing flashy—just quiet confidence, like the cards are politely saying, "I'm over here, he's over there."

Keep every other screen untouched.
title: UX: Mobile orders – cards feel undefined, hard to separate at a glance

body:
- Screenshot 1 (Android counting screen): labels, SKUs, quantity controls and text overlap/scramble (feels cramped and broken)
- Screenshot 2 (iOS counting screen): cleaner but still oversized and heavy
- Screenshot 3 (main Inventur list): cards and info overlapping/scrambled
- Bottom nav reappears and completely blocks 'Abbrechen' + 'Fertig' buttons in counting flow (both platforms)
- Whole Audit module feels too big — needs subtle zoom-out shrink for better breathing room and fit
- Keep global swipe-to-hide nav behavior, but protect content/action bar in Audit only so buttons are never covered

```bash
gh issue create --title "Audit: Android overlaps + bottom nav collision + shrink entire experience" --body "- Screenshot 1 (Android counting screen): labels, SKUs, quantity controls and text overlap/scramble (feels cramped and broken)
- Screenshot 2 (iOS counting screen): cleaner but still oversized and heavy
- Screenshot 3 (main Inventur list): cards and info overlapping/scrambled
- Bottom nav reappears and completely blocks 'Abbrechen' + 'Fertig' buttons in counting flow (both platforms)
- Whole Audit module feels too big — needs subtle zoom-out shrink for better breathing room and fit
- Keep global swipe-to-hide nav behavior, but protect content/action bar in Audit only so buttons are never covered" --label ux --label enhancement --label high-priority --milestone "Phase B: Product & UX Overhaul" --web
```

**Claude prompt (copy-paste ready):**

Design the entire Audit (Inventur) module so it finally feels calm, light, and perfectly at home on both iPhone and Android.

You have three screenshots in front of you — study them carefully so you can picture exactly what the user is seeing right now.

First screenshot (Android, item-counting screen inside an active audit):  
The user is in the middle of scanning and counting products. You see two product cards stacked vertically. Each card shows the SKU at the top (SB12-3.4… and SB12-12L…), the location name underneath, then the quantity row with minus, number (currently 0), plus, and the little copy and delete icons. Below the cards there is a thin summary bar that reads “2 Artikel · 0 Stück” on the left and two big buttons on the right: “Abbrechen” (gray) and “Fertig” (bright blue). Right now everything feels crowded — the text and controls look like they are overlapping or fighting for space, especially on Android. The cards feel too tall and tight, and the whole screen feels heavy.

Second screenshot (iOS version of the exact same counting screen):  
Same layout, same buttons, but the quantities are already at “3”. It looks a little cleaner than Android, yet the overall scale still feels too big and chunky — the fonts, cards, buttons and spacing are all a little oversized, like the design was made for a much larger screen.

Third screenshot (main Inventur overview / list screen):  
This is the first screen the user lands on when they tap “Inventur”. At the top you see the big “Neue Inventur” button, then below it the search field and filter tabs. Then comes the “VERLAUF” section with previous audit cards. On this Android screenshot the cards are visibly scrambled — text, dates, status badges, numbers and icons are piled on top of each other or misaligned, making the list feel messy and broken.

The bottom navigation bar at the very bottom of every screen (Lager • Artikel • Bestell. • Eingang • Inventur) behaves the same way across the whole app: swipe up and it gently disappears, swipe down and it slides back up. We want to keep this exact swipe behavior everywhere — it feels native and delightful. However, inside the Audit counting flow the problem is that when the nav reappears it overlaps and hides the “Abbrechen” and “Fertig” buttons. The user cannot tap them without first swiping the nav away again. That feels frustrating and unreliable.

What we need now is a warm, forgiving, perfectly proportioned Audit experience:

- In every screen inside the Audit module the scrollable content must end with a safe breathing space so that the action bar (“Abbrechen” / “Fertig”) always sits cleanly and permanently above the bottom navigation bar. The buttons should never be covered, even when the user swipes the nav back up. The content simply “knows” where the nav lives and respects that invisible line.

- Fix the Android layout scrambling completely. All text, SKUs, quantity controls, cards and labels must breathe with generous, consistent spacing — no more overlaps, no more squished elements. The iOS version should stay equally beautiful.

- Scale the entire Audit module down just a touch (imagine the user gently pinching to zoom out once on a webpage). Make every font a little more delicate, every card a little more compact, every button and icon perfectly proportionate, every piece of spacing refined and airy. The whole module should suddenly feel lighter, calmer and more spacious on both platforms — yet still perfectly readable and finger-friendly. Even the iOS version will benefit from this subtle refinement.

The user should open Audit, start counting, get interrupted, swipe the nav away and back, and never once worry about hidden buttons or messy text. Everything should feel confident, forgiving and quietly elegant.

Once you have implemented these changes, test the complete Audit journey on real iPhone and Android devices with sample data:

- Open the main Inventur list (third screenshot) — confirm cards are perfectly aligned and no longer overlapping.  
- Start a new quick audit and move to the counting screen — add several items, scroll, swipe the bottom nav up and down — confirm the “Abbrechen” and “Fertig” buttons stay fully visible and untouched at all times.  
- Check that the overall visual scale now feels lighter and more refined on both platforms.  
- Try sudden interruptions (background the app, rotate the device, come back) — everything must remain clean and accessible.

Keep every other part of the app completely untouched.
title: Audit: Android overlaps + bottom nav collision + shrink entire experience

body:
- Auto-save every product/quantity change as user scans—offline on device, online to Cosmos DB  
- On 'Cancel' or back button tap: show gentle modal 'Leaving? Your counts won't save—keep going later?' with 'Yes, save & exit' / 'No, finish now'  
- Unfinished audits appear in list with 'In progress' badge or subtle progress indicator  
- Restore exactly where left off after phone restart, app close, or navigation  
- Test with real interruptions: scan 5 items, exit, restart device—data persists  
- No banners or interruptions—just quiet, reliable saving



**Claude prompt:**

Design a gentle, forgiving way to handle interruptions in the Audit product-picking flow—on mobile, where real life happens.  

Right now, if someone taps "Cancel" or accidentally hits the back arrow while they're mid-scan, counting quantities, everything vanishes. They lose their work, get frustrated, and have to start over. That feels wrong—like the app doesn't trust them to step away.  

Instead: make it safe to pause. As soon as they start picking products (search, scan, enter qty), quietly save every change—offline on the device, online straight to Cosmos DB. No fuss, no "saving..." banner—just seamless.  

If they try to leave—whether by "Cancel," back button, or even a sudden phone restart—the screen should pause with a soft modal:  
"Leaving now? Your counts won't be saved—want to keep going later?"  
With two clear choices: "Yes, save & exit" or "No, finish now."  

Back in the main audits list, any half-done one should look unfinished—maybe a faint "In progress" badge, or a little progress bar—so they can tap back in and pick up exactly where they left off. No data lost. No restarts ruin anything.  

Keep it warm, human: like the app says, "Hey, I got you—go handle whatever, I'll hold this." Test it with real interruptions: scan five items, hit back, see the modal, choose save, restart the phone, come back—everything's still there. No overlaps, no glitches. Keep the rest of the flow untouched.
title: Audit: Auto-save in-progress counts + safe exit modal

body:
Add 'Project Tag' inline in order detail view—just like you did in the external reference:\n- Click to edit (single/multiple via chips/commas)\n- Save on blur/enter\n- No full PO edit—quick context only\n\nCopy existing external ref logic, rename & apply.

Prompt:

Hey Claude—quick add: project tag inline in order detail view.

Look at how external reference works right now: click field → edit inline (single or multiple via comma/chips), save instantly—no full PO modify.  

Do exactly the same for "Project Tag":  
- Click → editable input  
- Support one or many (e.g., "Alex, Team-B" as chips)  
- Save on blur/enter  
- Keep it visual, thumb-friendly—same style, no extra steps  

This is for context only—products stay in full edit. Test: add tag → see it show → edit again → no reload.  

Fast, clean—copy external ref logic, rename & apply.
title: Inline: Add project tag field to order detail (copy external ref style)

body:
Wareneingang desktop: keep step flow (PO → warehouse → items → recap), but make it feel like a workstation.

- Left fixed sidebar: PO #, type badge (blue Project/green Lager), external ref, project tag, warehouse, date.
- Vertical stepper (left or right): round dots, subtle progress—move from top-center.
- Warehouse picker: cards (Object/Service groups), tappable, finger-friendly for Surface.
- Main area: smooth item transitions (fade/slide).
- Recap: sidebar persists.

Use space—calm, immersive, no clutter.
title: Desktop: Refactor Wareneingang – sidebar context + vertical stepper + warehouse cards

body:
On mobile, Inspection flow loses context fast—no PO type, ref, tag, warehouse reminder.\n\nAdd fixed top bar (always visible):\n- 'Wareneingang – Lieferung 987654 – Lager Nord – 06.03.2026'\n- PO type badge: blue 'Project', green 'Lager'\n- External ref (if any): 'EXT-456'\n- Project tag: 'Tech: Alex' (assume live)\n\nAlso:\n- Drop search bar (PO fixed—no extras)\n- Soften swipe-to-next: gentle fade/slide, not snappy\n- Items = invisible steps (next = item, no confusion)\n- Recap: same header above list\n\nKeep product scroll smooth—no breaks.

Prompt:

Hey Claude—this is Wareneingang, one of our crown jewels. Treat it like the heartbeat of the app: every glance should feel certain, every tap effortless, every moment remembered.

Start with silence. After entering the delivery number and picking the warehouse, the screen shouldn't go blank—it should breathe. A fixed, velvet-thin header sits at the top: 'Wareneingang' in quiet type, then—PO 987654, date 06.03.2026, warehouse 'Nord'—all in one line, never scrolling away.  

Right beside: a tiny badge—emerald green for Lager (stock-bound), sapphire blue for Project (tagged work). If there's an external ref, it slips in like a footnote: 'EXT-456'. And the project tag—'Tech: Alex'—glows softly, so you never forget whose job this is.  

No search bar. We don't need it—PO's fixed, products are set. That space is now air, breathing room for the truth.  

Each item? A page turn, not a jump. Swipe left-to-right: gentle, almost liquid—fade in the next, ease out the old, like flipping a notebook page. No snap, no confusion—just 'oh, I'm here now'. Next button does the same, invisible step by step.  

Recap? Same header above the list—PO, type badge, ref, tag—like a bookmark. You walk away, come back, it's still yours.  

The scroll? Butter. Products glide under the header, never fight it. Shadows kiss the edges, light bends just enough—premium, not showy.  

This isn't a form—it's memory. Make it so smooth, so present, that users forget they're working. They just know: 'This is mine, this is now.'  

Put everything into this. Make it unforgettable.

labels.name: tech-debt
labels.description: Technical debt — should fix for reliability

title: Mobile Inspection UX: Add full context header + PO type badge in Wareneingang inspection

body:
Goal: dark/soft/light themes consistent everywhere—including login page.\n\n- On first load (unauthenticated): detect OS mode → apply matching theme.\n- After login: load saved user preference from theme OS.\n- If user changes theme in-app: save it → persist across sessions, including future logins.\n- No conflicts: user choice always wins over OS.

Prompt:

Hey Claude—let's make themes feel seamless, like they belong to the user from the first tap.

The login screen should greet them quietly: if their phone's in dark mode, ours is too—no flash, no jarring shift. It's the same calm they already know.  

Once logged in, the app inherits that choice. If they switch to soft or light later, we remember—save it, honor it. Even after logout, even after token expires, even at 2 AM in a warehouse with flickering lights: next open, same theme waits. No reset. No surprise white screen.  

User choice always trumps OS. System sets the starting point—then steps back. If they override, we lock it in, quietly, forever. No flicker on reload, no clash on relogin. Just continuity—like the app knows them better than they do.

Keep it effortless: no visible lag, no "loading theme" nonsense. The whole experience breathes together—polished, predictable, human-first.
title: UX: Make themes uniform across login + app, respect OS preference

body:
Add logout flow:\n- Top-right avatar: pull real photo from Azure AD (/me/photo).\n- Tap → dropdown → 'Logout' → clear token/session → redirect to login.\n- Offline: queue logout.\n- No fake registration—use existing MSAL/Expo auth.\n- Subtle UI, optional confirm.\n\nTest: pic loads, logout wipes data, no back access.\nQuick, reuse auth code.

Prompt:

Hey Claude—new feature: User logoff + profile picture from Azure

Build a clean logout flow:
- Top-right avatar (circular, 40px): Pull real profile pic from Azure AD/Microsoft Graph (use access token, endpoint /me/photo/$value or fallback to initials if no photo).
- On tap: Dropdown menu → "Logout" button.
- Logout: Clear local token/session (IndexedDB/localStorage), redirect to login screen (no back-button access).
- Handle offline: Queue logout if no net, sync on reconnect.
- No "simple app registration" — use existing Azure AD setup (MSAL.js or Expo AuthSession if mobile).
- UI: Subtle animation (fade out), confirm dialog optional ("Are you sure?").
- Test: Login → see pic → logout → back to login, no cached data.

Keep dark mode, offline-first.
title: Feature: User logout with Azure profile pic

body:
When tapping the bell icon at the top right, the notifications container appears misaligned and visually out of place.

**Android (1st screenshot):**
- The panel titled 'Benachrichtigungen' pops up as a floating box.
- It looks detached from the header, with awkward spacing and positioning.
- 'Keine Benachrichtigungen' text feels disconnected from the rest of the UI.

**iOS (2nd screenshot):**
- Same panel behavior.
- Slightly better alignment than Android, but still not perfectly integrated with the header (looks floating and inconsistent with the clean dark theme).

Expected result: The notifications panel should open cleanly and anchored directly under the bell icon, with proper shadow, rounded corners, and seamless integration on both platforms (like a native popover or dropdown).

Screenshots attached for reference.

Likely a CSS positioning / popover / z-index issue in the notification component.
title: Bug: Notifications panel misaligned/out of place when clicking bell icon (Android + iOS)

body:
Sidebar/nav (desktop & mobile): current order is Lager, Artikel, Bestellungen, Wareneingang, Lagerprotokoll, Lieferanten, Settings.\n\nMove Settings up—same level as other modules (not last). Applies to everyone, no admin check.\n\nQuick CSS/array reorder. Test: Settings icon appears before Lieferanten on all screens.

Prompt:

Hey Claude—small UX tweaks for the global Settings screen (mobile + desktop, same rules):

1. **Reposition Settings gear/icon**  
   - Current: Settings is always the last item in the bottom nav/sidebar (e.g. after Lager, Artikel, Bestellungen, Wareneingang, Lagerprotokoll, Lieferanten).  Leave the order of modules untuched in the button menu nav bar.
   - Wanted: Move it to the same visual level as the main modules—like Precoro style. So the nav becomes: Lager, Artikel, Inventur, Bestellungen, Wareneingang, Lieferanten, Lagerprotokoll, **Settings** (not buried at the end).  
   - If we add future modules, Settings should stay prominent—don't let it sink.  
   - Do this via CSS reorder (flex/order) or array change in nav component—keep it responsive (mobile: bottom bar, desktop: sidebar).  

2. **Remove amber warning message**  
   - Current: When entering global Settings, there's an amber banner saying something like "In the future, only administrators will be able to access this area".  
   - Wanted: Delete it completely—no point anymore. Permissions are already enforced (role-based), so it just clutters the UI.  
   - Remove the component or conditional render—clean slate.  

Test:  
- Open bottom nav → Settings should appear as a normal module, not last.  
- Click Settings → no amber box, just the options.  
- Add a dummy module later → Settings doesn't drop below it.  

Keep everything else (dark mode, toggles, etc.) untouched.
title: UX: Reorder sidebar/nav – move Settings up to module level

body:
Hey Claude—bug in Inventur counting screen (refactor already built):

Current behavior (bad):
- When scrolling products in counting screen, the bottom nav (log/audit/reports) hides or gets overlapped by content. Also when adding products on the audit, do not allow to repete products to be picked. Products must be picked just once.
- The progress counter ("7/8 products counted") and "Fertig" button get partially hidden behind the bottom nav. The progress counter should be on a more prominent place to be clearly seen, in a premium spot. People want to see how much work is left to go quickly to another task, so this is important.
- Top header (inventory title + search bar) scrolls up and disappears—loses context.

What we want (copy the warehouse screen's good effect):
- Bottom nav **always fixed** at bottom: position: fixed; bottom: 0; width: 100%; z-index: 999; no hide on scroll.
- Content (products list) scrolls **under** the bottom nav—like how the warehouse picker screen lets info slide under the fixed elements.
- Top header + search bar **fixed** at top: include title ("Inventur – Lager 3 – 05.03.2026"), user, progress ring, and search bar—never scrolls away.
- Products list: scrollable middle area, padded so nothing overlaps fixed parts (top padding = header height, bottom padding = nav height).
- When scrolling: products glide under header/nav like smooth glass—nice overlay effect (maybe subtle shadow or blur if possible).
- Thumb-friendly: big targets, no jank.

Why: People get distracted—need constant "where am I, how many left?" glance. Warehouse screen does it perfect—copy that magic.

Test: Start audit → add 10+ products → scroll up/down → header + nav stay, content slides under, counter visible.

Keep offline sync, scanner focus, blind mode untouched. Fix in CSS/layout
title: Bug: Bottom nav hides + header scrolls away in Inventur counting screen

body:
Add Settings → Audits: 'Force Normal Mode' toggle (default off).\n\n- On: Hide Quick Count on Screen 3 – only Normal card shows (auto-selected).\n- Off: Both cards visible.\n\nSave locally. Keeps supervised audits mandatory when needed.

Prompt:

Hey Claude—quick patch on the Inventur refactor (already built):

Add this to Settings → Audits section:

- New toggle: "Force Normal Mode" (default off)
- Description: "Disable Quick Count – all audits must be supervised (no shortcuts)"
- Behavior:
  - If on: On Screen 3 ("Wie?"), hide "Quick Count" card completely. Only show "Normal Inventur" card (auto-selected, no choice).
  - If off: Show both cards as before—user picks.
- Save setting to localStorage/IndexedDB (like blind mode).
- Test: Flip on → start new audit → only Normal card. Flip off → both back.

Keep everything else (sticky header, blind toggle, scanner prep) untouched.
title: Enhancement: Global toggle to force Normal/Supervised mode in audits

body:
Allow users to move stock from one warehouse/van to another.\n\n- Select source location → select target → choose products (search/grid) → quantity → confirm.\n- Track history: 'Transferred 50 bolts from Lager 1 to Van 4 – 05.03.2026'.\n- Offline support (queue for sync).\n- Add 'Urgent' flag/priority option.\n\nTest cases: partial transfer, zero qty, offline then sync.

Prompt:



**Feature: Stock Transfer Between Warehouses – Mobile-First, One-Screen Flow**

Build a new "Transfer Stock" screen. Goal: fast, no scroll (except products), feels like Apple Maps—simple, visual, no dropdowns. Fit everything on one portrait iPhone screen. Offline-first (IndexedDB + queue). Future scanner-ready (Eyoyo).  

**Layout (Fixed, No Scroll Except Products)**  
- **Top Bar (fixed, 80px)**:  
  - "Transfer Stock" (big title)  
  - "From: Lager 3 → To: Van 7" (bold center—your anchor)  
  - Right: "Urgent" toggle (Apple slider, red glow if on)  
  - Tap "From" or "To" → opens horizontal swipe carousel of locations (20 max, icons + name, current glows).  

- **Products Area (middle, scrollable only here)**:  
  - Big focused search bar: "Scan or type product"  
  - **Two modes (toggle at top: "Scan All First?" – default off)**:  
    - Off: Scan → auto-add card → qty stepper focused (+/− big, tap num-pad) → scan again → repeat. Auto-focus qty after add.  
    - On: Scan multiple → cards stack with 0 qty → tap card to fill qty (or bulk +/−).  
  - Cards: name/SKU + qty + delete X. Vertical stack, scroll if >4.  
  - Scanner prep: `addItem(sku)` reusable (from Inventur) – if qty mode, focus stepper; if bulk, add empty.  

- **Bottom Bar (fixed)**:  
  - Summary: "3 items • 150 units"  
  - Big green "Confirm Transfer" button → opens recap sheet (modal):  
    - "From Lager 3 to Van 7"  
    - List: "50 bolts", "100 screws"  
    - "Transfer Now" → saves to IndexedDB, logs to Laga protocol ("Transfer #TX-001: 50 bolts from Lager 3 to Van 7 – 05.03.2026 14:45"), queues sync.  

**Notifications**  
- On confirm: Local notification (Expo/Capacitor):  
  - Title: "Transfer Done"  
  - Body: "Transferred {qty} {product} from {from} to {to} – {date} {time}"  
  - Sound: subtle, badge +1  
  - Immediate (even app closed).  
- If "Urgent" on: Queue team push (Firebase/Expo – mock for now): "URGENT TRANSFER: 50 bolts from Lager 3 to Van 7".  

**Other**  
- Auto-save draft if interrupted (IndexedDB).  
- Reopen → loads last transfer.  
- Thumb-friendly: big targets, micro-animations (card pop, slider snap).  
- No back button—edit From/To inline.  

Start with layout + scanner loop + local notification. Test offline. Let me know when ready.
title: Feature: Stock transfer between warehouses

body: In light mode, some text (labels/buttons) is white-on-white → invisible. Happens randomly across app (e.g. headers, forms). Works fine in dark/soft. Fix: adjust contrast, maybe use #333/#000 for text. Test on bright screen.
title: Light mode text visibility bug: white text on white background

body:
Goal: Replace crowded start screen with 3-step glide.

- Screen 1: Pick location (grid of cards, fixed top/bottom).
- Screen 2: Auto-name 'Inventur – Lager X – DD.MM.YYYY' (editable).
- Screen 3: Quick/Normal cards.

Sticky top: always shows location + date + user + progress.

Future: scanner-ready (focus qty after add).

See prompt for full details:

No—it doesn't. The last prompt I gave you is the core one for start flow + header + cart. I left blind mode out because we were still deciding on it.

But here's the **updated version** with blind mode fully baked in (global + per-audit). Copy-paste this instead:

---

**Inventur – UX Refactor: Start Flow + Sticky Header + Shopping Cart Style + Blind Mode**

We already have the existing code. Refactor **only** the Inventur module start and counting screens.  

### Main Goals
- Clean, 3-step launch (no more crowded page)  
- Sticky top header for context  
- Shopping-cart counting  
- Blind mode: global override + per-audit toggle  
- Scanner prep (Eyoyo)

### 1. New 3-Step Start Flow
**Screen 1 – "Wo zählen?"**  
- Fixed top: "Inventur starten" + hint  
- Middle: Grid cards (locations, icons, last date)  
- Bottom: "Weiter" (disabled till pick)

**Screen 2 – Name**  
- Fixed top: "Inventur – Lager 3" + date/time + user  
- Middle: Auto-name "Inventur – Lager 3 – 05.03.2026" (editable)  
- Bottom: "Weiter"

**Screen 3 – Audit Type**  
- Fixed top: Full context  
- Middle: Two cards – Quick (green) / Normal (blue)  
- Below cards: **Per-audit toggle** "Blind Count? (Hide expected values)" – Apple slider, default off  
  - **Only show if global blind is off** (see below)  
- Bottom: "Starten"

### 2. Global Blind Mode (in Settings)
- New section "Audits"  
- Toggle: "Blind Mode Permanent" (default off)  
- Text: "Hide expected stock in all audits – overrides per-audit choice"  
- If on: per-audit toggle disappears, blind auto-active (no expected shown till end)  
- If off: per-audit toggle visible on Screen 3

### 3. Counting Screen
- Fixed top (1/6): "Inventur – Lager 3 – 05.03.2026" + user + progress "12/30"  
- Middle: Empty cart → search/add → card with stepper (+/−)  
- Swipe left: note, right: next  
- After qty: auto-focus search (scanner-ready)  
- Bottom: "Abbrechen" / "Fertig" (diff summary)

### 4. Other
- Bottom nav fixed (no hide)  
- Offline auto-save (IndexedDB)  
- Thumb-friendly, animations, premium feel  

Start with start-flow + sticky header + blind logic. Test offline.
title: Refactor Inventur start flow: grid + sticky header + auto-name

body:
**Issue:** Entra ID app client secret was exposed in terminal output during debugging session.

**Action:** Go to Azure Portal → Entra ID → App registrations → ProcureFlow → Certificates & secrets → New client secret. Update Key Vault secret `EntraClientSecret`.
title: SECURITY: Rotate Azure Entra ID client secret

body:
**Issue:** Cosmos DB primary key was exposed in terminal output during debugging session.

**Action:** Rotate the key in Azure Portal → Cosmos DB → Keys → Regenerate Primary Key. Then update the Key Vault secret `CosmosDbConnectionString` with the new connection string.
title: SECURITY: Rotate Cosmos DB connection key

body:
**Problem:** Feature-based module visibility hides nav items, but a user could still programmatically navigate to hidden modules (e.g., by manipulating state or using browser dev tools).

**Fix:** Add a guard in `handleNavigation()` that checks `userHasAccess(module)` before allowing navigation. Already partially implemented — needs to return early or redirect to dashboard.
title: Navigation guard for hidden modules

body:
**Problem:** All Azure Functions API endpoints accept arbitrary JSON bodies without validation. Any shape of data can be written to Cosmos DB.

**Impact:** Malformed data could corrupt the database. No protection against accidental or malicious payloads.

**Fix:** Add `zod` schema validation to all POST/PUT handlers. Define schemas matching TypeScript interfaces in types.ts.

labels.name: tech-debt
labels.description: Technical debt — should fix for reliability

title: K22: No input sanitization on API endpoints

body:
**Problem:** Both handlers read `purchaseOrders.find(...)` outside the `setState` callback — the same stale closure bug that was fixed for `handleReceiptSuccess`.

**Impact:** API may persist outdated order data if the order was modified between render and click.

**Fix:** Move the `find()` and `ordersApi.upsert()` calls inside the `setState(prev => { ... })` callback.
title: K21: handleArchiveOrder / handleCancelOrder read stale closures

body:
**Problem:** `handleFileUpload` in SettingsPage uses `id: raw['Artikel Nummer'] || 'generated-id-' + index` — no dedup suffix. Duplicate Artikel Nummer values produce duplicate IDs.

**Fix:** Use `crypto.randomUUID()` or align with the data.ts dedup pattern.
title: K19: SettingsPage import creates non-unique IDs

body:
**Problem:** `archivedReceiptGroups` is written by App.tsx to localStorage, then read by ReceiptManagement via a `focus` event listener. This is fragile — depends on timing and browser focus behavior.

**Fix:** Lift into App.tsx React state, pass as prop to ReceiptManagement, persist to API.

labels.name: tech-debt
labels.description: Technical debt — should fix for reliability

title: K16: archivedReceiptGroups synced via localStorage

body:
**Problem:** `syncFromApi()` uses `if (data.stock.length > 0)` to guard state updates. If a collection is legitimately empty (e.g., all orders archived), the state won't update — stale data persists.

**Fix:** Change to `if (data.stock !== undefined)` or `if (Array.isArray(data.stock))`.
title: K15: syncFromApi won't sync empty collections

body:
**Problem:** `loadAllData()` in api.ts only fetches stock, orders, receipts, tickets, audits. The `deliveryLogsApi` and `suppliersApi` exist in api.ts but are not called during app boot.

**Impact:** Delivery logs and supplier data only available if manually fetched. Not cached in IndexedDB.

**Fix:** Add both to the `Promise.all` in `loadAllData()`. Update `AppData` interface. Add to `cacheAllData()` and `getCachedAppData()` in offlineDb.ts.
title: K13: delivery-logs and suppliers not loaded on mount

body:
**Problem:** Several data types are only in localStorage, not persisted to Cosmos DB:
- `lagerortCategories` (warehouse location groupings)
- `auditTrail` (system action log, max 500)
- `stockLogs` (stock movement log, max 500)
- `notifications` (in-app notifications, max 50)
- User preferences (theme, view mode)

**Impact:** Data lost on browser clear. Not available on other devices. No server-side backup.

**Fix:** Create Cosmos DB `user-prefs` container (partition /userId). Sync on write + load on mount.

labels.name: tech-debt
labels.description: Technical debt — should fix for reliability

title: K10: localStorage as source of truth for some data

body:
**Problem:** `/api/health` uses `stripMeta()` but all other endpoints return raw Cosmos documents with `_rid`, `_self`, `_etag`, `_attachments`, `_ts` fields.

**Impact:** Frontend `cleanDocs()` handles it, but adds unnecessary payload size on every request.

**Fix:** Add `stripMeta()` to all endpoint handlers (stock, orders, receipts, tickets, delivery-logs, suppliers). The audits endpoint already has it — use as reference.

labels.name: tech-debt
labels.description: Technical debt — should fix for reliability

title: K9: Only /api/health strips Cosmos metadata server-side

body:
**Problem:** Single `receipts` container holds ReceiptMaster, ReceiptHeader, ReceiptItem, and ReceiptComment documents, distinguished only by `docType` field.

**Impact:** Cross-partition queries get expensive at scale. All queries must filter by docType.

**Fix:** Split into separate containers: receipt-masters, receipt-headers, receipt-items, receipt-comments. This is a breaking change requiring data migration + API + frontend updates.

**Container:** receipts (/poId)

labels.name: tech-debt
labels.description: Technical debt — should fix for reliability

title: K8: Receipts container holds 4 doc types

body:
**Problem:** 182 duplicate Artikel Nummer values in seed data. Cosmos keeps last-write-wins (~613 unique docs out of 795).

**Impact:** Stock items may be silently overwritten. Affects ~23% of seeded data.

**Fix:** UUID-based IDs or composite keys for production data. Run dedup script against live Cosmos container.

**Container:** stock (/id)

labels.name: tech-debt
labels.description: Technical debt — should fix for reliability

title: K6: Duplicate SKU IDs in seed data
